question.text: Dividend received by a REIT from an SPV that has opted for Section 115BAA (concessional tax regime) is treated how for unit-holders?

answer.text: Taxable in the hands of unit-holders
Taxable at the REIT level and exempt for unit-holders
Taxable at a flat rate of 10% for unit-holders with no further liability

explanation.text: **The Logic:** When the SPV has opted for the concessional tax regime under Section 115BAA, dividend from that SPV distributed through the REIT becomes taxable in the hands of unit-holders. The exemption under Section 10(23FD) is NOT available in this case. The dividend is still exempt at the REIT level, but the unit-holder must pay tax on it. **The Trap:** Section 10(23FD) exemption for unit-holders is the biggest trap here. This exemption ONLY applies when the SPV has NOT opted for 115BAA. If the SPV enjoys the lower corporate tax rate under 115BAA, the trade-off is that unit-holders lose the dividend exemption. **Cross-Exam Context:** Section 115BAA was a key Budget change; NISM Series X-B tests whether candidates understand the cascading impact of SPV-level tax elections on unit-holder taxation. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always check whether the REIT's underlying SPVs have opted for 115BAA before advising clients on after-tax returns. This one factor can significantly change the effective yield from REIT distributions.
